coredata.dc:title: Light on multicolor fluorescent carbon dots from a single biomass precursor through simply tuning the solvothermal temperature and time strategy
coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Fu
Bai, Jianliang
He, Pinyi
Wang, Xinyu
Wu, Shuang
Yu, Xu
Yao, Yongkang
Ren, Lili
coredata.dc:description: The conversion of biomass to carbon-based nanomaterials has recently received increasing attention, thanks to in line with the ideas of environmental protection and sustainable chemistry. Up to now, facial and fast synthesis of a single biomass precursor-derived carbon dots (CDs) with tunable multicolor fluorescence remains a challenge. Herein, we select mosses as cheap, abundant, and renewable biomass to prepare CDs with multicolor fluorescence by simply tuning solvothermal temperature and time. Through the characterization and comparison of a series of moss-derived CDs, it is revealed that the reaction temperature and time can effectively control the ratio of chlorophyll as the red luminescence center and carbon-core states as the blue luminescence center. Moreover, strong emission in the deep red light region, low toxicity, and outstanding biocompatibility made m-CD180 suitable for cellular imaging. This study provides a simple method for the green utilization of mosses and also provides a new idea for the fluorescence regulation and application of biomass-derived CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass
Carbon dots
Multicolor fluorescence
Sustainable chemistry
Waste-to-wealth

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of moss ethanol extract/moss ethanol solution

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22275035).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of moss ethanol solution, red and blue fluorescent CDs.

(a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis of moss ethanol solution and multi-color CDs. (b) FL spectra of moss ethanol solution and various CDs under 400 nm excitation. (c) The corresponding CIE color coordinates of moss ethanol solution and various CDs.

(a)–(d) UV/vis absorption, FL excitation spectra and FL emission spectra of moss ethanol solution, m-CD180, m-CD200, and m-CD240, respectively. (e)–(h) FL spectra of moss ethanol solution, m-CD180, m-CD200, and m-CD240 at different excitation wavelengths.

TEM images of (a) m-CD180, (b) m-CD200, and (c) m-CD240 and inset photographs of corresponding CDs solution under 365 nm UV light. Corresponding dots diameter distribution histograms of (d) m-CD180, (e) m-CD200, and (f) m-CD240.
(a) Raman spectra, (b) FT-IR spectra, and (c) XPS scan spectra of moss powder (green line), m-CD180 (red line), m-CD200 (cyan line), and m-CD240 (blue line).
Proposed possible mechanism of the multi-color fluorescence.
Laser confocal fluorescent images of cells incubated with m-CD180.

head.ce:title: Light on multicolor fluorescent carbon dots from a single biomass precursor through simply tuning the solvothermal temperature and time strategy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 211189, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 211189, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The conversion of biomass to carbon-based nanomaterials has recently received increasing attention, thanks to in line with the ideas of environmental protection and sustainable chemistry. Up to now, facial and fast synthesis of a single biomass precursor-derived carbon dots (CDs) with tunable multicolor fluorescence remains a challenge. Herein, we select mosses as cheap, abundant, and renewable biomass to prepare CDs with multicolor fluorescence by simply tuning solvothermal temperature and time. Through the characterization and comparison of a series of moss-derived CDs, it is revealed that the reaction temperature and time can effectively control the ratio of chlorophyll as the red luminescence center and carbon-core states as the blue luminescence center. Moreover, strong emission in the deep red light region, low toxicity, and outstanding biocompatibility made m-CD180 suitable for cellular imaging. This study provides a simple method for the green utilization of mosses and also provides a new idea for the fluorescence regulation and application of biomass-derived CDs.

Using mosses as cheap, abundant, and renewable single biomass source to prepare a series of CDs with multicolor fluorescence by simply tuning solvothermal reaction conditions was successfully achieved. Because of the excellent optical properties in the deep red light region and biocompatibility, the CDs with red fluorescence were used as nanoprobes for live-cell imaging without any chemical modifications.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of fluorescent nanomaterials, have received considerable attention and research from many scholars owing to their excellent fluorescence properties, solubility, and environmental friendliness [1–4]. They are potential candidates for a variety of modern nanotechnologies (fluorescence detection [5,6], bioimaging [7,8], diagnosis and therapy [9,10], light-emitting devices [11], information encryption [12,13] and photocatalysis [14,15]). In recent years, breakthroughs have been made in the syntheses of multicolor CDs due to their excellent versatility. Several different multicolor CDs have been synthesized with various precursors such as citric acid and urea [16], phenylenediamine [17], dihydroxybenzene [18], etc. Conventional methods for the synthesis of CDs usually require toxic organic small molecules or polymers as precursors, thus increasing environmental and energy consumption problems [19]. Therefore, the development of low-cost, widely used, and eco-friendly CDs derived from biomass raw resources is the current prevailing direction [20]. In recent years, various biomass precursors, such as leaves [21–24], grass [25], eggs [26], hairs [27], fruit [28–30], and vegetables [31,32] have been developed to prepare CDs. It should be noted that compared with the development trend of CDs in full swing, biomass-derived CDs have more complex luminescence mechanisms. The reason lies in the more complex biochemical structures of their precursors, which makes the CDs’ structure generated more difficult to understand. In addition, few reports concern the multicolor fluorescence of CDs derived from a single biomass carbon source due to hard realization. However, this single-precursor-triggered multicolor fluorescence is fascinating because it can make the production process cost-effective and beneficial. Hence, more endeavors are still highly needed for biomass-derived CDs, for instance, to explore a simple solvothermal strategy to achieve multicolor fluorescence of CDs [33,34].

Our previous research found that when the reaction conditions were mild, the leaves-derived CDs showed red fluorescence (FL) mainly caused by the chlorophyll molecular state because of the protection of the biological microenvironment, while when the reaction conditions became harsh and the chlorophyll structure was destroyed, the CDs might show blue fluorescence mainly caused by carbon core. Thus, the fluorescence emission of biomass-derived CDs may be modulated by tuning the state of chlorophyll in biomass. Among the many biomass precursors, mosses are one of the most abundant plant species, and peatlands (land with naturally accumulated layers of moss) cover about 4 million square kilometers (about 3 % of the Earth's area) [35]. In addition, moss has a low degree of differentiation and relatively strong cell growth potential energy, so it can keep the plant evergreen all year round [36,37]. These unique advantages make moss a nice candidate for synthesizing carbon dots. Another very important reason why we chose moss as a biomass precursor is that moss is rich in chlorophyll, which is beyond the reach of ordinary leaves or grass. And it is well known from our previous research that chlorophyll-rich substances can be a good potential candidate for the preparation of deep red CDs [19]. Therefore, we envisage whether it would be possible to prepare multi-color fluorescence biomass-derived CDs by simply modulating the reaction conditions (Fig. 1 ).

Based on the above considerations, here, we report a solvothermal synthesis of CDs with multicolor fluorescence emission from a single carbon source of mosses. The detailed comparative experiments show that the FL emission of mosses-derived CDs is closely related to chlorophyll molecules and carbon-core states, which can be adjusted by reaction time and temperature. On this basis, the photoluminescence mechanism and the formation process of CDs are proposed. Because of the excellent optical properties in the deep red light region and biocompatibility, the CDs with red fluorescence were used as nanoprobes for live-cell imaging without any chemical modifications. This work paves the way toward the green synthesis of multicolor FL CDs from a single biomass carbon source, which will greatly stimulate the development of CDs through a nontoxic, low-cost, and eco-friendly pathway for various potentials.

The mosses were picked at Southeast University (Nanjing, China). Anhydrous ethanol (AR) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (China). Deionized water (18.1 MΩ cm) was obtained using the Milli-Q water purification equipment.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a Talos F200X microscope operating at 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a Bruker Tensor 27 ATR-FTIR spectrometer with KBr pellets. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out with a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI instrument. UV–vis absorption spectra were performed with a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were collected using a Horiba Fluoromax-4 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Raman spectra were obtained on a Horiba Scientific LabRAM HR Evolution via a microspectrometer with an excitation wavelength of 532 nm laser. PL lifetimes were collected on PluoroLog 3-TCSPC. Absolute QY was measured on PluoroLog 3-TCSPC with an integrating sphere. Cell imaging was carried out using a confocal laser fluorescence microscope (STELLARIS5, Leica, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of moss ethanol extract/moss ethanol solution
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh mosses were first cleaned to remove mud, cut off the roots, kept only the leaves and stems, dried in an oven at 60 °C for 2 h, and ground into powder while still hot. 0.02 g of the above moss powder was added to 6 mL of anhydrous ethanol to make a moss ethanol solution.

0.10 g mos powder was scattered in 30 mL anhydrous ethanol and transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) lined autoclave (50 mL). The solvothermal reaction was performed in an oven at 180 °C for 6 h (named m-CD180). After cooling down to room temperature, the products were purified by silica column chromatography using ethanol as the eluent. The solvent removal via rotary evaporation and drying under vacuum at 80 °C for 48 h. 0.10 g smashed moss powder and 30 mL anhydrous ethanol were added into a 50 mL autoclave and heated to 200 °C for 6 h (named m-CD200). 0.07 g of smashed moss powder and 20 mL of deionized water were transferred into a flanged stainless steel reactor liner (25 mL) and heated to 240 °C for 6 h (named m-CD240). The subsequent purification and drying steps for m-CD180 were equally applicable to m-CD200 and m-CD240.

0.10 g mos powder was scattered in 30 mL anhydrous ethanol and transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) lined autoclave (50 mL). The solvothermal reaction was performed in an oven at 160 °C for 6 h (named m-CD1). 0.10 g smashed moss powder and 30 mL anhydrous ethanol were added into a 50 mL autoclave and heated to 180 °C for 8 h (named m-CD2). 0.10g smashed moss powder and 30 mL anhydrous ethanol were transferred into a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) lined autoclave (50 mL) and heated to 200 °C for 10 h (named m-CD3). 0.10 g mos powder was scattered in 30 mL anhydrous ethanol and transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) lined autoclave (50 mL). The solvothermal reaction was performed in an oven at 200 °C for 12 h (named m-CD4). 0.07 g of smashed leaves and 20 mL of deionized water were transferred into a flanged stainless steel reactor liner (25 mL) and heated to 220 °C for 6 h (named m-CD5). After the m-CD1, m-CD2, m-CD3, m-CD4, and m-CD5 solutions were cooled to room temperature, the subsequent purification and drying steps were as shown above, respectively. 0.1 g of moss powder was extracted with ethanol to form moss ethanol extract, which was concentrated to 30 mL and reacted in a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) lined autoclave (50 mL) at 180 °C for 6 h to prepare ml-CD180. The subsequent purification and drying steps were as shown above.

HeLa cells were first seeded into a 96-well plate at a density of 5 × 104 cells mL−1 and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. After the original medium was removed, the cells were incubated with different concentrations of m-CD180 (0, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100 μg mL−1) for 24 h. Subsequently, 20 μL MTT (5.0 mg mL−1 PBS) solution was added to each well. After 4 h incubation, the growth medium was again removed and 150 μL of DMSO was then added to dissolve MTT. Finally, a microplate reader was used to record the optical density of each sample at a wavelength of 490 nm after shaking for 10 min at room temperature. Control experiments without the m-CD180 were also performed. Each experiment was conducted three times, and the averaged data were presented.

HeLa cells were seeded into a 24-well cell culture dish containing DMEM supplemented with 10 % FBS and 5 % CO2 at 37 °C for 24 h. Then, we removed the medium and washed the cells with PBS (pH = 7.4) three times. Then, 60 μg mL−1 m-CD180 solution was added to the culture medium and incubated for 4 h. Afterwards, the cells were treated again with PBS buffers to remove the remaining probes. The cells were observed via a confocal microscope with a 405 nm laser.

Fresh mosses were first cleaned to remove mud, cut off the roots, kept only the leaves and stems, dried in an oven at 60 °C for 2 h, and ground into powder while still hot. The above-milled powder of mosses was directly dispersed in anhydrous ethanol to form the ethanol solution which also emitted extremely deep red fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation (Fig. 1). The optical properties of the ethanol solution were characterized, and both the UV/vis and fluorescence spectra are in almost perfect agreement with the spectra of the leaves-derived CDs previously studied by our group (Fig. 3a and e) [19]. According to the above assumption, we modulated the fluorescence by changing the temperature and time of the solvothermal reaction. It is worth noting that mosses as precursors show a good prospect of fluorescence regulation. And we successfully obtained the multi-color fluorescence from red to near white to blue (Fig. 2 a). The fluorescence spectra of these species (Fig. 2b) showed a remarkable and regular change with temperature and time. As the temperature and time increased, from the excitation of independent dark red fluorescence of moss ethanol solution to the appearance of double emission peaks, the red fluorescence peak at ∼670 nm gradually weakened, the blue fluorescence peak at ∼480 nm gradually strengthened, and finally turned to only blue fluorescence peak. The detailed optical features are shown in Figs. S1–S5. The change of the ﬂuorescent color is presented in a CIE chromaticity coordinate (Fig. 2c). Along with the increase of reaction temperature and time, the luminescence color jumped from red (0.71, 0.28) to near white (0.32, 0.35) to blue (0.21, 0.30) [38]. These multicolor fluorescence CDs are successfully fabricated which offer great potential in practical lighting applications.

First, we investigated the effect of reaction time on fluorescence emission. The reaction temperature was fixed at 180 °C, and the reaction time was changed for 4 h, 10 h, and 12 h, respectively. The fluorescence spectra are shown in Fig. S6. When the reaction time was reduced, it was definite that the intensity of red fluorescence increased compared to that of blue fluorescence. In particular, when the reaction time reached 10 h and then extended, the fluorescence spectra did not change significantly, indicating that at 180 °C, even an extended reaction time was not sufficient to destroy the red fluorescent molecules and that the solvothermal temperature is the key factor in fluorescence modulation. Therefore, to further investigate the mechanism of multicolor luminescence, three representative CDs obtained by the mosses at solvothermal temperatures of 180 °C, 200 °C, and 240 °C were selected and named m-CD180, m-CD200, and m-CD240, respectively (see the experimental section and Fig. 2a). The crude products were purified by silica column chromatography using ethanol as the eluent. UV/vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of moss ethanol solution, m-CD180, m-CD200, and m-CD240 were first investigated to evaluate their optical properties. It has been reported that the absorption peaks of CDs at 200–250 nm indicate the typical absorption behavior of the π-π* transition of the aromatic system. In the spectra of the moss ethanol solution, there is a strong absorption peak at ∼406 nm and a weak absorption peak at 630–670 nm, which generally correspond to the Soret and Q bands of the chlorophyll-derived porphyrins, respectively (Fig. 3a) [39]. When the emission wavelength is 676 nm, the peak of the corresponding excitation spectrum coincides with the absorption peak at ∼406 nm, indicating that the fluorescence of the moss ethanol solution comes from chlorophyll (Fig. 3a). As the temperature rises from room temperature to 200 °C, the characteristic absorption peak of chlorophyll gradually becomes weaker (Fig. 3b-c). At the same time, the peaks of the corresponding excitation spectra overlapped with the absorption peak at ∼406 nm, indicating that the red fluorescence of m-CD180 and m-CD200 was derived from chlorophyll. When the temperature increases to 240 °C, the characteristic absorption peak of chlorophyll disappears completely, which points that the chlorophyll structure is destroyed (Fig. 3d). These features are well reflected in the fluorescence spectra (Fig. 3f–h). With the increase in temperature, the excitation-independent red fluorescence component at ∼670 nm weakens, and the excitation-dependent blue fluorescence component at ∼480 nm appears and gradually increases. The luminescent center gradually shifts from the red fluorescence of chlorophyll to the blue fluorescence of the carbon core.

More importantly, this strategy can be easily expanded to other chlorophyll-rich biomasses, such as bamboo leaves, clovers, and camphor leaves. The powder of bamboo leaves, clovers, as well as camphor leaves, was prepared as CDs at 180 °C, 200 °C, and 240 °C, respectively, but the other conditions were the same with m-CDs. As shown in Fig. S7, the fluorescence of these three chlorophyll-containing biomasses gradually shifted from red to blue as the solvothermal temperature increased. These results demonstrate that the present work provides a universal green and simple approach to fabricating multicolor CDs from a single biomass source.

To explore the microstructure of these prepared m-CDs, TEM and Raman spectroscopies were performed. Fig. 4 shows the representative TEM images of as-prepared samples. It can be seen that all the materials are almost dot-shaped. According to the size distribution diagrams, these three samples present average diameters of 2.0 nm (m-CD180), 2.6 nm (m-CD200), and 1.5 nm (m-CD240). The intensity ratio of the D band at 1370 cm−1 to the G band at 1638 cm−1 (ID/IG) is 0.78, 0.77, and 0.84 for m-CD180, m-CD200, m-CD240, respectively (Fig. 5 a). As it is known, the value of ID/IG stands for graphitization degree and the ratio of sp3/sp2 carbon [40]. The ID/IG value presents a slight decline and then an upward trend, which means that the graphitization content increases first and then decreases from 180 °C to 240 °C. In addition, 2D bands appear in their Raman spectra, indicating that these CDs formed were stacked in multiple layers. With the increase in temperature, part of the chlorophyll (with porphyrin structure) was destroyed to form a carbon core, and the degree of graphitization increased. Later, as the chlorophyll structure was destroyed more, the degree of graphitization began to decline. FT-IR analyses were performed to characterize the surface functional groups of the as-prepared CDs. The detailed results are displayed in Fig. 5b. The main characteristic peaks of the FT-IR spectra of these CDs are broadly consistent. The broad absorption bands are observed at ∼3420 cm−1, which is assigned to the stretching vibrations of N–H and O–H. Additionally, the peaks in the range of 2840–2950 cm−1 are corresponded to the vibrations of –CH2-, indicating the existence of hydrophobic long alkyl chains on the surface (Fig. S8). In particular, significant absorption differences can be observed in the FT-IR spectrum of m-CD240. The characteristic peaks at 900-500 cm−1 in m-CD240 are attributed to the C–H bending vibration of the aromatic ring. The peaks at ∼1740 and ∼1150 cm−1 that are attributed to CO and C–O–C of the ester group fade away with the increase in temperature. The intensity of the peaks at ∼1380 and 1460 cm−1 corresponding to –CH3- and –CH2- are decreased sharply. It is particularly noteworthy that the peak intensity of CN/CC from the porphyrin ring at 1650 cm−1 is greatly weakened in m-CD240, which may be due to the destruction of porphyrin ring structure on the surface and further participation in the formation of carbon core due to the increase of temperature, which can be known by XPS. Therefore, the elemental composition and surface chemical states of these CDs are further studied by XPS. The results from the XPS full scan spectra (Fig. 5c) indicate that all these CDs mainly consist of C, N, and O. The relative atomic contents of different elements are listed in Table S1. The high-resolution C 1s spectra (Figs. S9–S12) exhibit three carbon species, including C–C/CC at ∼284.4 eV, C–N/C–O at ∼285.7 eV and CO/CN at ∼286.5 eV. The N 1s spectra of three CDs are resolved into two peaks located at ∼399.5 and ∼400.4 eV, related to pyrrolic N and graphic N, respectively [41]. The O 1s XPS spectra of three CDs contain two peaks at ∼532.1 and ∼533.2 eV attributed to CO and C–O, respectively [42]. Although the relative content of C–C/CC in the C1s spectrum decreased from 85.83 % to 84.44 %–81.21 % for m-CD180, m-CD200, and m-CD240, the absolute content of C–C/CC showed a slight increase followed by a decrease from 69.07 % to 69.95 %–60.88 %, respectively (Table S2). This result is in good agreement with the Raman analysis, reflecting the dynamic change of the fluorescence center of these CDs with the temperature change.

To further investigate the possible mechanism of the multi-color fluorescence, comparative experiments were carried out. The moss ethanol extract was directly added to anhydrous ethanol and ml-CD180 was synthesized after solvothermal reaction at 180 °C for 6 h to explore the formation process of multicolor CDs. Different from the solvothermal reaction of moss powder with ethanol, the main component of moss ethanol extract is chlorophyll. After the solvothermal reaction, the red fluorescence peak at ∼670 nm in its fluorescence spectrum completely disappears, and only blue fluorescence appears (Fig. S13), which is consistent with the general understanding that the structure of chlorophyll will break down at a certain temperature. While, for m-CD180 with red fluorescence prepared by moss powder, the original biological structure of moss can protect chlorophyll from being destroyed at relatively mild temperatures, which is consistent with our previous research phenomenon [19].

In addition, time-resolved PL spectra were employed to understand the exciton dynamics of moss ethanol solution, m-CD180, m-CD200, and m-CD240, and the results are shown in Fig. S14. The fluorescence lifetime of moss ethanol solution was fitted to a single exponential curve with 5.46 ns, indicating that it has only one fluorescence center. For m-CD180, it shows largely bimodal emission, one fluorescence peak of ∼480 nm was fitted to a bi-exponential curve with an average lifetime of 3.57 ns, probably assigned to the core state [43], and the other fluorescence peak of ∼670 nm was fitted to a mono-exponential curve with a fluorescence lifetime of 6.12 ns, indicating only one red fluorescence center [23]. The m-CD200 also exhibits bimodal emission, and the lifetime of the blue fluorescence part is 2.92 ns, while that of the red fluorescence part is 5.73 ns, which is roughly consistent with the red light lifetime of moss ethanol solution and m-CD180. The time-resolved PL spectrum of m-CD240 displays a multi-exponential decay with an average lifetime of 3.25 ns, which is consistent with the blue light portion of m-CD180 and m-CD200 [44]. It is further confirmed that the chlorophyll molecule emitting red fluorescence is confined to the carbon dots structure from moss powder, and the blue fluorescence is derived from the intrinsic carbon nuclear state formed at higher solvothermal temperatures. Furthermore, to elaborate on the possible microscopic states of prepared CDs, we also compared the stability of moss ethanol solution, m-CD180, m-CD200, and m-CD240. Fig. S15 shows the fluorescence spectra of the moss ethanol solution and the prepared CDs before and after 150 days of storage respectively. After 150 days of storage, the fluorescence intensity of the moss ethanol solution was reduced remarkably (Fig. S15a), which may be because the chlorophyll is unstable and easy to decompose after leaving the biological microenvironment, leading to the reduction of its fluorescence intensity. It is worth noting that m-CD180 and m-CD200 show enhanced emission intensity after 150 days of storage compared to day 1 (Figs. S15b–15c), which may be related to the formation of hydrogen bonds between CDs nanoparticles or between chlorophyll in the restricted CDs domain with other CDs, leading to the crosslinking enhancement effect (CEE) and thus enhanced fluorescence intensity and stability [45]. Furthermore, Fig. S15d shows that m-CD240 stored for 150 days showed a relatively same intensity of emission spectrum. Based on these results, we propose a possible formation process as in Fig. 6 . One of the most important features for applications in the biological field is the fluorescence quantum yield of CDs. Fig. S16 shows the absolute quantum yields of the moss ethanol solution, m-CD180, m-CD200, and m-CD240. The moss ethanol solution rich in chlorophyll exhibited a good quantum yield of approximately 11.7 %, while the quantum yield of m-CD180 was 12.1 %. The enhanced quantum yield once again confirms that CEE may be the cause. However, the QY of bimodally emitting m-CD200 is about 3.4 % in the red fluorescence and less than 1 % in the blue fluorescence. The QY of m-CD240 with blue fluorescence was measured to be 5.3 %. The above results indicate that the prepared CDs have remarkable photostability, good quantum yields, and long fluorescence lifetimes, making CDs an effective fluorescent probe for live cell imaging, which may be useful in microscopy and analysis.

Considering the superior optical properties of the as-prepared CDs, we attempted to apply m-CD180 to biological imaging. Before biological application, the potential cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of the m-CD180 were evaluated against HeLa cells by MTT assay at first [43]. As presented in Fig. S17, more than 95 % of HeLa cells were viable after 24 h incubation with m-CD180 at concentrations of 10–100 μg mL−1. These achievements prove that our CDs can be safely applied as fluorescent probes in the field of cellular imaging. The HeLa cells were cocultured with the m-CD180 (60 μg mL−1) at 37 °C for 4 h and then subjected to confocal fluorescence imaging (Fig. 7 ). When these HeLa cells were excited at 405 nm wavelength, they emitted bright red emissions, which indicates that the m-CD180 are capable of passing through the cell membranes, getting into the cells, and labeling the HeLa cells successfully [46].

In conclusion, we have developed a green and simple strategy for the solvothermal synthesis of multicolor CDs with moss as a single carbon source. By simply adjusting the temperature and time of solvothermal reaction, the ratio of chlorophyll as the red luminescent center to carbon nucleus as the blue luminescent center was effectively controlled, and multicolor CDs from red to near white to blue fluorescence were prepared. The formation process and photoluminescence mechanism of CDs were proposed. In addition, m-CD180 with deep red fluorescence exhibits favorable biocompatibility and high stability, providing a very effective fluorescent probe for cell imaging in vitro.
Fu Qin: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing-Original Draft, Writing-Review & Editing, Visualization.
Jianliang Bai: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Review & Editing.
Pinyi He: Formal analysis, Investigation.
Xinyu Wang: Methodology, Investigation.
Shuang Wu: Methodology, Investigation.
Xu Yu: Methodology, Investigation.
Yongkang Yao: Formal analysis, Investigation.
Lili Ren: Conceptualization, Validation, Formal analysis, Review & Editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22275035).

J. Am. Chem. Soc.
